title: Stillingia brasiliensis
taxonomy: Stillingia brasiliensis is classified in the family Euphorbiaceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as the spurge family. This family is part of the order Malpighiales, which includes a wide range of plants that are primarily found in tropical and subtropical regions. The Euphorbiaceae family is characterized by its unique flowers that produce a milky sap, often referred to as latex. Stillingia brasiliensis, as a member of this family, shares these characteristics and is a native plant to the tropical regions of Central and South America. Further classification of Stillingia brasiliensis includes its genus and species levels, which are Stillingia and brasiliensis, respectively.

intro: Stillingia brasiliensis, also known as "palo de lumbre," is a plant species native to the tropical regions of Central and South America, belonging to the family Euphorbiaceae. The plant has been traditionally used to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin infections, due to its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antipyretic properties. The bioactive constituents of Stillingia brasiliensis include stilbenes, flavonoids, and triterpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. Traditionally, the plant is used to make herbal preparations such as teas, tinctures, and infusions, and is also used in the production of natural remedies for pain relief and fever reduction.
